claims.text: Mysterious 'sky trumpet' sounds — eerie, sometimes musical, sometimes animalistic — have been recorded by ordinary citizens around the world (Houston, Argentina, Denmark, Singapore, New Zealand, Ireland, California, San Antonio, Czech Republic) since at least 2012, with no agreed-upon scientific explanation.

evidence.quote:
just before midnight on March 17th
2020 the people of houon woke up to Erie
sounds that seem to be coming from the
[Music]
sky

this sound was heard all over
the world Denmark Singapore New Zealand
Ireland California really everywhere

claims.rationale: The 'sky trumpets' phenomenon is well-attested in news coverage and online videos from many countries since around 2008-2012. Whether the recordings are all the same phenomenon and whether some are hoaxes / wind effects / synthesized soundtracks is unsettled. The host accurately reports the geographic spread.

claims.text: 'Skyquakes' — cannon-like booms heard on clear days from Seneca Lake to global locations — have been documented since at least 1850 (Cooper's 'Guns of Seneca' / Washington Irving's 1819 Catskills account), and remain unexplained but plausibly attributable to shifting tectonic plates or magnetic-field events.

evidence.quote:
in rip
vanwinkle which was published in 1819
author Washington Irving references
thunder-like sounds in the Catskill
Mountains

The Unexplained booms near Sena
Lake are so commonly heard they've got
their own name The Guns of senica
they've been heard since at least 1850

claims.rationale: The Guns of Seneca / Lake Seneca booms are a real and long-documented phenomenon. Irving's 'Rip Van Winkle' (1819) does reference mysterious booms in the Catskills. Iroquois oral tradition concerning sky-noise is also documented. The host is accurate on the historical attestation.

claims.text: The Taos Hum — a low-frequency rumble that emerged in Taos, New Mexico in 1991 — was studied by Los Alamos, Sandia, the Phillips Air Force Lab, and the University of New Mexico but no acoustic, seismic, or electromagnetic source was ever identified, and only about 2% of people can hear it (including some deaf individuals).

evidence.quote:
in 1991 the residents of TOS New Mexico
were shaken out of their sleep by a
strange humming sound

high-tech
equipment was set up at a few locations
where the hum was prevalent scientists
collected acoustic geodynamic seismic
magnetic and electromagnetic
measurements but even with all this
modern technology the study was unable
to find the source of the to's hum

strangest of all only about 2% of people
could actually hear it these unlucky few
call themselves The
[Music]
hearers

claims.rationale: The 1993 Taos Hum study by these institutions (Mullins, Kelly et al.) is real and did fail to find a source. The ~2% audibility figure is widely cited though varies by survey. The 'even some deaf people hear it' detail is more anecdotal and harder to verify. Core claim is solidly grounded.

claims.text: The Windsor Hum — emerging in Windsor, Canada (with 22,000 complaints in a single day) and credited to industrial activity on Zug Island — exemplifies how mysterious hums often have mundane industrial sources but the Windsor case still doesn't fully explain the 2%-only audibility pattern.

evidence.quote:
during a single day 22,000 people
complained about the hum researchers
think the sound was coming from nearby
Zug Island where there's a lot of
industrial activity but there's been
industry on the island for a 100 years
the hum is new and it doesn't explain
why only 2% of people could hear it

claims.rationale: Windsor, Ontario complaints about a low hum traced to Zug Island industrial sources are documented (the Canadian government commissioned a study around 2014). The host's residual puzzle (why only some hear it) is a real open question.

claims.text: Underwater hydrophones have detected several sounds whose sources remain unidentified — including the seasonal 'Upsweep' (1991, mid-Pacific), the loud 'Bloop' (1997, South Pacific) audible across thousands of miles, and the metallic 38 Hz 'Western Pacific Biotwang' (2014, Mariana Trench).

evidence.quote:
in 1991 scientists discovered an
unexplained recurring sound known as the
upsweep

in 1997
scientists picked up a strange sound
coming from from the South Pacific Ocean
this one they called the
bloop

a short 2 to 4 second complex
sound was recorded that features a 38 HZ
moan with both harmonics and amplitude
modulation followed by broad frequency
metallic sounding sweeps up to 7.5 khz
this sound was recorded regularly during
both fall and spring
surveys they call it the Western Pacific
biot twang

claims.rationale: Upsweep, Bloop, and Western Pacific Biotwang are all real NOAA hydrophone discoveries. The Bloop has since been attributed largely to icequakes. Biotwang has been linked to minke whale calls (per the host's later note). Upsweep remains contested. Reasonably accurate.

claims.text: The Wow! Signal — a 72-second, 30-standard-deviation narrowband radio burst at the hydrogen line (1420 MHz) detected by Ohio State's Big Ear telescope on August 15, 1977 by astronomer Jerry Ehman — is still the strongest single piece of evidence we have for intelligent extraterrestrial life.

evidence.quote:
it was the evening of August 15th
1977 the sky over Ohio was clear as
astronomer Jerry aan sat in his basement
office scanning radio signals from deep
space

on August 15th Big Ear picked
up a signal that registered
6eq
J5 that is an unbelievably intense
reading it lasted for 72 seconds and
peaked at an intensity 30 standard
deviations above the sound setting
normally captured

this was so amazing that
Jerry circled the string of data and red
and wrote wow next to it it's been known
as the wow signal ever since

claims.rationale: The Wow! Signal is correctly described: detected August 15, 1977 by Jerry Ehman at the Big Ear telescope, 72 seconds long, narrowband near the hydrogen line, never reproduced. The 30-sigma figure refers to the intensity above background. The host's framing as our strongest single SETI candidate is widely shared.

claims.text: The 2017 'comet' explanation for the Wow! Signal — that comets 266P/Christensen and P/2008 Y2 (Gibbs) emitted hydrogen passing through the beam — has been dismissed because the timing and location don't actually match.

evidence.quote:
in 2017 a scientist claimed it was
caused by one of two comets that were
known to be moving through the area at
the time but this theory was eventually
dismissed because the timeline and
location just didn't line up

claims.rationale: Antonio Paris's 2017 cometary-hydrogen hypothesis was indeed published and received attention, then was substantially debunked by other astronomers (Robert Dixon and others) on the grounds that the comets weren't in the correct position and don't emit at the required intensity. Host's dismissal matches the consensus.

claims.text: A US Geological Survey 2011 report attributes most skyquakes to mundane sources — shallow earthquakes, mining-induced rock bursts, large offshore waves, or wind-shifted sand dunes — and many low-frequency hums have prosaic industrial explanations.

evidence.quote:
according to a
2011 report from the US Geological
Survey Sky Quakes the rumbles heard on a
clear day could be caused by shallow
earthquakes or spontaneous Rock bursts
triggered by mining or from giant waves
breaking offshore or Loosely packed sand
dunes reshaped by strong winds

in the hums usually have industrial
explanations building air vents
high-press gas lines high tension power
lines heavy machinery even Sonic booms
and oil Derek but these don't explain
all of them but they do explain most

claims.rationale: USGS has discussed natural and anthropogenic sources for booming-sand and skyquake-style sounds. The industrial-source explanation for many local hums (Bristol Hum, Largs Hum, Auckland Hum) is well established. Host's framing — most explained, some not — is correct.

claims.text: The host's verdict frames the universal human fascination with end-of-world signals (trumpets, hums, skyquakes) as a coping mechanism — preparing emotionally for catastrophe — rooted in a 'survivor gene' shared across cultures and faiths.

evidence.quote:
people in all cultures regardless
of Faith have a fascination with
apocalyptic scenarios and we always have
there are many reasons for this religion
the media and just Natural Curiosity but
I think we're obsessed with the end of
the world because of a single Primal
reason control thinking about the
apocalypse allows us to prepare mentally
for the possibility

now I bet this is something
hardwired in our DNA by our creator by
God the simulation or whatever you want
to call it it's some kind of survivors
Gene

claims.rationale: This is the host's editorial closing argument — explicitly framed as opinion / personal philosophy with the Murakami quote and the personal anecdote about his father. I omit directTruth because it is a normative/psychological speculation rather than a falsifiable claim about external phenomena.
